title-group.article-title: Demographic Variation in Social Support and Intimate Friend Across 22 Countries: A Cross-National Analysis

abstract.p: Prior research documents strong associations between social support and having an intimate friend with improved health and well-being outcomes, with most of the evidence based on data from the U.S. However, less is known about how these indicators of having close social connections differ across countries and across demographic groups within those different countries. The development of targeted, culturally appropriate interventions around close social connections requires understanding how they vary across countries and demographic groups. This study presents an in-depth, cross-national exploration of social support and having an intimate friend and its variations across key demographic groups. Using a diverse and international dataset of 202,898 individuals from 22 countries, this paper examines relationships between levels of social support, having an intimate friend, and key demographics, including: age, gender, marital status, employment status, education, and immigration status. The descriptive results present the ordered means of social support and the ordered proportion of having an intimate friend across countries. Globally, most people report having an intimate friend (84%) and people who will help them (7.4 on scale of 0–10), both of which, however, vary greatly across countries, and highlight who does not. They also vary greatly, overall, by employment status, educational level, and religious service attendance. This work illuminates the distributions and descriptive statistics of social support and having an intimate friend across these demographic features and offers insight into country-specific variations that lay a valuable foundation for future investigations into sociocultural influences that might shape close social connections.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1007/s41042-025-00226-0.

funding-source.institution: Funding for this research was provided by the John Templeton Foundation Templeton, the Templeton World Charity Foundation, Templeton Religion Trust, the Fetzer Institute, the David & Carol Myers Foundation, the Paul Foster Family Foundation, Well-Being for Planet Earth, and the Well Being Trust.

sec.title: Close Social Connections Are Key Determinants of Human Flourishing
sec.p: We study close social connections (CSC) because they are a key determinant of human flourishing (Holt-Lunstad, 2022; Lomas, 2022; Waldinger & Schulz, 2023). This research conceptualizes CSC as including two circles of expanding emotional connection (Dunbar, 2018), with an inner circle of people with whom we feel very close (intimate friend) and a broader circle of relatives and friends who will support us when we are in trouble (social support). These CSC influence human flourishing, defined as whole-human well-being, where all aspects of a human’s life are good (Lee et al., 2021; VanderWeele et al., 2023; VanderWeele, 2017a). Longitudinal studies across 105 countries find CSC to be one of the strongest predictors of happiness (Helliwell et al., 2010, 2023), “approximately equivalent to a fivefold increase in household income” (Helliwell & Aknin, 2018, p. 248). This research uses a dataset from 22 countries to show that these flourishing-critical CSC vary by demographic category and by country. The rest of this Introduction briefly highlights the literature showing how CSC levels influence human flourishing.

sec.title: Addressing a Global Epidemic of Declining Close Social Connections
sec.p: People around the world experience loneliness and a lack of CSC with negative consequences for the individuals and their communities (CDC Social Connection, 2024; Ernst et al., 2022; Gallup & Meta, 2023; Luhmann et al., 2023). Governments have created structural responses to pervasive deficiencies in CSC (U.S. Surgeon General, 2023; World Health Organization, 2024), such as the installation of Ministers of Loneliness in the UK and Japan (Goldman et al., 2024). Understanding how and why CSC are dwindling requires understanding how CSC vary across countries and across demographic groups within those countries (Case et al., 2023; Henrich et al., 2010). This study describes the demographic distribution of levels of CSC within each of the surveyed countries, the variance in which demographic groups do well in CSC across countries, universal similarities that support existing theory, and national differences that invite further theoretical development.

sec.title: Close Social Connections
sec.p: Prior research has investigated the structure and characteristics of close social connections. For example, research has conceptualized CSC in terms of the size or diversity of one’s social network and the frequency of social interactions (Helliwell & Putnam, 2004; Knack & Keefer, 1997; Piao et al., 2022a, 2022b; Sampson et al., 1997). Along these lines, the quality or depth of CSC has been defined in terms of whom individuals call in their social network for help (Ajrouch et al., 2010; Collins et al., 1993; Ellison & George, 1994; Julien & Markman, 1991; Merino, 2014; Untas et al., 2011). Other research has focused on the affective qualities that define CSC, such as relationship satisfaction, cohesion, intimacy, closeness, strain, conflict, trust, and participation (Holt-Lunstad, 2022; Veenstra, 2002). Dunbar (2018) characterizes the forming of CSC in circles of increasing emotional closeness, with a broader circle of relatives and friends who will show up when we are in trouble (social support) and an inner circle with whom we deeply confide (intimate friend). Studies find that CSC, as specifically defined here, are valued across all ages and all countries (Gallup, 2021; Hofstede et al., 2010; Waldinger & Schulz, 2023).

sec.title: Close Social Connections and Flourishing
sec.p: Study of the relevance of CSC for human flourishing dates back to at least Durkheim (1897) who speculated that aspects of modern civilization undermined the development of social connections and broader social integration into communities, with negative implications for individual vitality and longevity. In the middle of the twentieth century, European sociologists developed the concept of social networks to better understand the impact on individual and societal wellbeing of social ties that extended beyond those of kinship, location, or class (Berkman & Glass, 2000; Festinger, 1954). Contemporary theorists have been able to tie CSC to positive health benefits (e.g., DuPertuis et al., 2001; Harvey & Alexander, 2012; Holt-Lunstad, 2022; Kawachi & Berkman, 2001; Kumar et al., 2012; Umberson & Donnelly, 2023).

To further nuance the nature of these social connections, research finds impacts on well-being through both the strong social ties of CSC and the weak social ties of social networks. For example, “strong ties” research finds that the depth of strong ties CSC are the most substantive contributor to lifelong physical and mental health (Waldinger & Schulz, 2023), and that strong CSC generally promote health by providing emotional support, reducing stress, and encouraging healthy behaviors (Antonucci et al., 2019; Marsden, 2024). “Weak ties” research shows that loneliness and health behaviors like smoking cessation and exercise spread through a broader circle of social networks that strengthen financial stability and cross-network information flow, often leading to significant health impacts (Aral et al., 2009; Cacioppo et al., 2009). To this growing research deepening the definition of the impact of social ties on well-being, this study examines two questions from the GFS to describe the demographic distribution of strong ties CSC across 22 culturally diverse countries.

The impact of CSC on flourishing is also supported globally, with the Gallup World Poll data from 142 countries finding that CSC are a strong predictor of overall well-being (Helliwell et al., 2023). An implication of this body of research is that declining CSC pose dangers to individual flourishing across the globe, based mostly on empirical evidence from data in the U.S. Given suggestive evidence that CSC are unevenly distributed across countries and across demographic groups within countries (Gallup & Meta, 2023), it is likely that the risks to individual well-being are more acute in certain demographic groups and parts of the world.

sec.title: Demographic Characteristics and Close Social Connections
sec.p: This study explores how CSC vary across countries and key demographic groups, including age, gender, marital status, employment status, education, religious service attendance, and immigration status. Previous research provides a baseline for this exploration, as indicated below, but to date there has not been a systematic assessment of how CSC vary across countries and across these demographic categories.

A Gallup World Poll analysis of 1.2 million individuals from 162 countries, from 2009–2019, found that social well-being increases with age (Shiba et al., 2022). Killen and Macaskill (2020) found that CSC were the most valued activity when asking adults over 60 years old to record three events that went well during the day. Age is theorized to be positively associated with social support in how people learn to structure their social network as they age by deepening high-CSC relationships (Carstensen, 2006); similarly, maturity supports the formation of intimate friendships by virtue of older adults minimizing their exposure to negative CSC and choosing their intimate friends from the many people they have met along the way (Antonucci et al., 2013). This literature shows that CSC associate positively with age.

On average, women tend to have fewer friends than men but those relationships tend to be more intimate (Roy et al., 2022). Some research suggests that women may be more likely than men to both give and receive emotional support, but such gender differences are less clear for instrumental support (Liebler & Sandefur, 2002). The benefits of gender for forming and maintaining CSC flow from gender differences in reproductive strategies (Palchykov et al., 2012) and in a woman’s preference for dyadic relationships versus a man’s preference for larger coalitions (David-Barrett et al., 2015).

Being married is positively associated with perceived social support (Boersma & Vahratian, 2021; Marks & Lambert, 1998). Research attributes this association between marital status and social support to having a built-in support network with a committed partner (Shapiro & Keyes, 2008) who provides emotional and practical support (An et al., 2024).

Prior work suggests that an individual’s employment status is associated with CSC, in part because work organizations provide opportunities to forge connections with coworkers. In empirical work in Japan, workers identify the workplace as an environment where they can receive social support (Piao & Managi, 2022; Piao et al., 2022a, 2022b). Other research suggests that job displacement is linked with having fewer CSC (Brand & Burgard, 2008), and the strength of the link between unemployment and lower engagement in social interactions has been shown to vary when compared across several European countries (Dieckhoff & Gash, 2015). The literature finds CSC to be positively associated with being employed.

Previous literature surveys in the United States show that education increases pro-social behavior and may increase access to CSC (Stevens et al., 2008). As the educational level of the mother increases, children are more likely to live with two adults (Hout, 2012), doubling their exposure to CSC. Education tends to develop communication skills for deepening relationships, access to a wider diversity of perspectives, and access to broader social networks (Park et al., 2018). The literature associates CSC positively with educational level.

Empirical work has identified religion as a possible source of social support (Diener et al., 2011; Nooney & Woodrum, 2002; Paranjape & Kaslow, 2010), with potentially positive effects of social support due to religious coping (Krause et al., 2001; Prado et al., 2004; Smolak et al., 2013) and opportunities to aid others (Krause et al., 1999). Moreover, religious beliefs also may be associated with perceived social support as a result of a sense of belonging to a religious community (Ellison & George, 1994; Le et al., 2016; Merino, 2014). Longitudinal studies associate religious service attendance with an increased likelihood of making new friends and higher social support (Li et al., 2018; Lim & Putnam, 2010; Strawbridge et al., 1997; VanderWeele, 2017b).

Success in immigration depends on social connections to secure and maintain employment and to adapt to the culture in the new country. Interpersonal ties and network connections play an important role in the immigration process, by providing immigrants with the social capital necessary to facilitate employment in the new locale (Massey et al., 1993). CSC have been shown to act as a crucial resource for counteracting immigration stress in a sample of undocumented Latino immigrants in the United States (Sanchez et al., 2019). On balance, the literature shows CSC to be positively associated with being an immigrant.

sec.title: Variability Across and Within Countries
sec.p: While a broad literature shows significant cross-cultural variability in the determinants of general social well-being (Case et al., 2023; Henrich, 2020; Lomas, 2018; Ritchie-Dunham, 2023) and specifically for loneliness (Hong et al., 2023) and social support (Diener & Tay, 2015; Höltge et al., 2022), less is known about how levels of CSC differ across countries and across demographic groups within those different countries. We expect significant variability in the levels of CSC across demographic categories and across countries.

sec.title: The Present Study
sec.p: Prior research has established (1) strong associations between CSC and improved well-being, (2) that socioeconomic conditions and social structures significantly influence how people form and maintain CSC, and (3) that these socioeconomic conditions can vary by country. To develop targeted, culturally appropriate interventions for CSC, ultimately enhancing health and well-being globally, it is crucial to understand how CSC vary across countries and demographic groups. However, there is limited knowledge about whether meaningful variations exist in CSC across sociodemographic groups and countries. To address this gap, the present study uses the GFS dataset to describe the mean and distribution of CSC by demographic characteristic across and within these 22 culturally diverse countries.

The pre-registered research questions driving this paper (Ritchie-Dunham et al., 2023b) are:
What are the distributions and descriptive statistics of key demographic factors (age, gender, marital status, employment, education, immigration status) in our diverse, international sample across 22 countries?
For Social Support:
How do mean levels of social support order across different countries?
How do levels of social support vary across different demographic categories such as age, gender, marital status, employment, education, and immigration status?
For Intimate Friend:
How does the proportion having an intimate friend vary across different countries?
How does the proportion having an intimate friend vary across different demographic categories such as age, gender, marital status, employment, religious service attendance, education, and immigration status?

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: The description of the methods below has been adapted from VanderWeele et al. (2025). For comparability across the set of studies analyzing all of the GFS items, a common set of methods was developed. To support other researcher in extending the GFS, much greater detail is provided in companion papers on the overall study (Johnson et al., 2024), the GFS methodology (Ritter et al., 2024), the selection of the GFS items (Lomas et al., 2025), how each item was tested (Markham et al., 2024), the survey sampling design (Padgett et al., 2025b), analytical methodology (Padgett et al., 2025a), and the statistical analysis code (Padgett et al., 2024).

sec.title: Study Population
sec.p:
The Global Flourishing Study (GFS) is concerned with the determinants of well-being and consists of a sample of 202,898 participants from 22 geographically and culturally diverse countries (see Fig. 1), with nationally representative sampling within each country. Wave 1 of the data included the following countries and territories: Argentina, Australia, Brazil, Egypt, Germany, Hong Kong (S.A.R. of China, with mainland China included from 2024 onwards), India, Indonesia, Israel, Japan, Kenya, Mexico, Nigeria, the Philippines, Poland, South Africa, Spain, Sweden, Tanzania, Turkey, United Kingdom, and the United States. The countries were selected to (a) maximize coverage of the world’s population, (b) ensure geographic, cultural, and religious diversity, and (c) prioritize feasibility and existing data collection infrastructure. Data collection was carried out by Gallup Inc.Fig. 1
22 Countries in global flourishing study

Data for Wave 1 were collected principally during 2023, with some countries beginning data collection in 2022 and exact dates varying by country (Ritter et al., 2024). Four additional waves of panel data on the participants will be collected annually from 2024–2027. The precise sampling design to ensure nationally representative samples varied by country and further details are available in Ritter et al. (2024). Survey items included aspects of well-being such as happiness, health, meaning, character, relationships, and financial stability (VanderWeele, 2017a), along with other demographic, social, economic, political, religious, personality, childhood, community, health, and well-being variables. The data are publicly available through the Center for Open Science (COS, https://www.cos.io/gfs). During the translation process, Gallup adhered to TRAPD model (translation, review, adjudication, pretesting, and documentation) for cross-cultural survey research (Mohler et al., 2016).

sec.title: Outcome Variables
sec.p: The two outcome variables describe the level of CSC. Using the Dunbar framing of circles of embrace, the Intimate Friend item asks if there is someone, yes or no, that is very close to the respondent, with whom they can confide and share their feelings, which we frame as Dunbar’s innermost circle of embrace. This suggests whether this very intimate social network—the innermost circle—exists (1) or not (0). The Social Support item asks about the frequency of support from a wider circle of embrace that includes relatives and friends, ranging from 0 = never to 10 = always. This question does not specify the size of the wider circle of Social Support, rather the frequency with which relative and friends will provide support.

To determine whether someone has an intimate friend, this study modified the Nurses’ Health Study (Nurses’ Health Study II (long), 2021) question: “Is there any one special person you know that you feel very close to? For example, someone you can confide in and share your feelings with. (If you have more than one person who you feel very close to select “Yes.”) [Y/N]. The analyses dichotomized this as yes [1] vs no [0]. This question has been used to explore social support in the context of physical and mental health (Kroenke et al., 2006; Michael et al., 2000; Trudel-Fitzgerald et al., 2016).

The level of social support is assessed with a modified version of the Gallup World Poll question: “If you were in trouble, how often could you count on people in your life, like relatives or friends, to help you whenever you need them?” [0 = Never, 10 = Always], assessed as a continuous outcome (Markham et al., 2024). This question has often been used to assess social support with multinational data (Diener & Tay, 2015; Helliwell et al., 2009; Oishi & Diener, 2014; Takahashi et al., 2018).

Having the two outcome variables describes the level to which people report having a very close circle (Intimate Friend) and a more extended circle (Social Support) and the distribution of the existence of these two circles across demographic groups.

sec.title: Demographic Variables
sec.p: Continuous age was classified as 18–24, 25–29, 30–39, 40–49, 50–59, 60–69, 70–79, and 80 or older. Gender was assessed as male, female, or other. Gallup historically uses the term gender with the terms male and female (Marlar & Willcoxon, 2023). To be consistent with the actual framing Gallup used and across the many GFS-related papers, the tables and online supplements used here retain the original text Gallup of gender, female and male. To be consistent with APA recommendations on reporting gender (American Psychological Association, 2019), throughout this text we change the terms males to men and females to women. Marital status was assessed with six categories: single/never married, married, separated, divorced, widowed, and domestic partner. Employment status was assessed as employed, self-employed, retired, student, homemaker, unemployed and searching, and other. Education was assessed as up to 8 years, 9–15 years, and 16 + years. Religious service attendance was assessed as more than once/week, once/week, one-to-three times/month, a few times/year, or never. Immigration status was assessed dichotomously with: “Were you born in this country, or not?” Religious tradition/affiliation had response categories of Christianity, Islam, Hinduism, Buddhism, Judaism, Sikhism, Baha’i, Jainism, Shinto, Taoism, Confucianism, Primal/Animist/Folk religion, Spiritism, African-Derived, some other religion, or no religion/atheist/agnostic; precise response categories varied by country (Johnson et al., 2024). Racial/ethnic identity were assessed in some, but not all, countries, and response categories were unique to each country. For additional details on the assessments see the GFS codebook (Gallup, 2024) and Markham et al. (2024).

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p: Descriptive statistics for the full sample, weighted to be nationally representative within each country, were estimated for each demographic variable. Nationally representative means/proportions for each outcome were estimated separately for each country and ordered from highest to lowest along with 95% confidence intervals. For the continuous social-support variable, country-specific standard deviations and Gini coefficients are reported. For the binary intimate-friend variable, the standard error is reported. Variation in means for each outcome across demographic categories were estimated, with all analyses initially conducted by country (online supplement).

Primary results consisted of a random-effects meta-analysis of country-specific means of each outcome in each specific demographic category (Borenstein et al., 2010; Hunter & Schmidt, 2000), along with 95% confidence intervals, standard errors, upper and lower limits of a 95% prediction interval across countries, heterogeneity (τ), and I2 for evidence concerning variation within a particular demographic variable across countries (Mathur & VanderWeele, 2020). Random-effects meta-analysis was chosen to pool estimates across countries because the analysis assumes a common underlying construct, which is a weaker assumption than strict measurement invariance assumed by multilevel modeling. The common underlying construct is a tenable assumption because all responses used the same single-item assessment and cognitive testing showed no major concerns in cross-cultural interpretation. The resulting country-specific estimates for each demographic category form a distribution of means/proportions, not samples from an identical superpopulation, which can be combined and summarized. Forest plots of estimates are available in the online supplement. All meta-analyses were conducted in R using the metafor package (Viechtbauer, 2010). Within each country, a global test of variation of outcome across levels of each particular demographic variable was conducted, and a pooled p-value (Wilson, 2019) across countries was reported concerning evidence for variation within any country. Bonferroni corrected p-value thresholds are provided based on the number of demographic variables (Abdi, 2007; VanderWeele & Mathur, 2019). Religious affiliation/tradition and race/ethnicity were used within country as control variables, when available, but these coefficients themselves were not included in the meta-analyses since categories/responses varied by country. As a supplementary analysis, population-weighted meta-analyses of the regression coefficients were estimated. All analyses were pre-registered with COS prior to data access, with only slight subsequent modification in the regression analyses due to multicollinearity (Ritchie-Dunham et al., 2023a); the code to reproduce analyses is openly available in an online repository (Padgett et al., 2024).

sec.title: Missing Data
sec.p: Missing data on all variables was imputed using multivariate imputation by chained equations, and five imputed datasets were used (Acock, 2005; Rubin, 1996; Sterne et al., 2009; van Buuren, 2018). To account for variation in the assessment of certain variables across countries (e.g., religious affiliation/tradition and race/ethnicity), the imputation process was conducted separately in each country. This within-country imputation approach ensured that the imputation models accurately reflected country-specific contexts and assessment methods. Sampling weights were included in the imputation models to account for specific-variable missingness that may have been related to probability of inclusion in the study.

sec.title: Accounting for Complex Sampling Design
sec.p: The GFS used different sampling schemes across countries based on the availability of existing panels and recruitment needs (Padgett et al., 2025b; Ritter et al., 2024). All analyses accounted for the complex survey design components by including weights, primary sampling units, and strata. Additional methodological detail, including accounting for the complex sampling design, is provided elsewhere (Padgett et al., 2025a).

sec.title: Demographic Variability
sec.p:
Table 1 shows the nationally representative descriptive statistics of the observed sample for 202,898 people from 22 countries. Age ranged from 18 to over 80, distributed in similar proportions in each age group. Gender was 51% female, 48% male, and 0.3% identifying as other. Participants who were married comprised 53% of the sample, with 2.6% separated, 5.7% divorced, 4.8% widowed, 7.4% being domestic partners, and 26% never married. Most participants worked for an employer (39%) or were self-employed (18%), with 14% retired, 5.3% students, 11% homemakers, and 8.3% unemployed. A majority of participants attended religious service, from 13% at least once a week, to 19% once a week, 9.7% 1–3 times a month, and 20% a few times a year and 37% never attend. Participants’ education varied: 22% had up to 8 years of education, 57% 9–15 years, and 21% 16 years or more. A majority of participants were not immigrants, with only 4.8% reporting that they were born in another country than where they were living at the time of the survey. Table 1

While the distributions for age and gender were consistent across countries, by design, the demographic categories of life circumstances for CSC (e.g., marital status, employment, religious service attendance, and education) varied greatly across countries. While the distribution of marital status was generally similar across countries, in some countries the percentage of married varied significantly, from 77% in India to 20% in South Africa, and the percentage of those never married doubled to 59% in South Africa, while the domestic partners tripled to 22% in the Philippines. While employment status was fairly consistent across countries, in some countries the percentage of self-employed tripled to 51% in Tanzania and 57% in Nigeria, and the unemployed quadrupled to 38% in South Africa. Religious service attendance varied widely for those never attending from 77% in Japan to 3% in Tanzania. Education varied greatly for those with 16 + years from 52% in Israel to 1.3% in Tanzania. Being born in another country varied from 0.1% in the Philippines to 23% in Australia and 24% in Israel.

sec.title: CSC Variability
sec.p: From the random-effects meta-analysis, the overall mean for social support is 7.40 [95% CI: 7.02,7.78] and overall proportion for intimate friend is 0.83 [95% CI: 0.81,0.86]. The population-weighted meta-analysis mean for social support is 6.25 [95% CI: 6.14,6.36] and proportion for intimate friend is 0.80 [95% CI: 0.79,0.82]. While this confirms that most people report having social support when they are in trouble and having an intimate friend, the mean levels of these indicators of CSC vary greatly across surveyed countries. To delve into the variability of CSC for each country surveyed, we report on the means and statistical dispersion of CSC, how the two indicators of CSC relate, and differences within demographic categories.

Table 2 shows the mean level of social support and the proportion of people reporting having an intimate friend in the 22 countries surveyed. The mean level of social support varies from a low in Japan [mean = 5.29, 95% CI: 5.25, 5.33, SD 2.37] to a high in Israel [mean = 8.55, 95% CI: 8.42, 8.68, SD 2.06]. The proportion of people having an intimate friend varies from a low in Japan [proportion = 0.67, 95% CI: 0.66, 0.68] to a high in Nigeria [proportion = 0.93, 95% CI: 0.917, 0.938]. The variability of social support within countries can also vary greatly, such as in Egypt [mean = 6.92, SD 3.61]. The standardized mean differences between groups from the outcomes is provided in the forest plots in the online supplement. Table 2

Note. N = 202,898. 95%CI = 95% confidence interval for the mean. SD = standard deviation of the value for each country. Gini = Gini coefficient index of inequality, a measure of statistical dispersion, where perfect equality = 0.0 and inequality = 1.0. SE = standard error of the value for each country. The combination of a small value for SE and rounding can lead to nonsymmetric CI for some countries

The levels of the two CSC outcome variables are similar in some countries and different in others (Fig. 2). While Israel has higher mean scores on both and Japan has lower mean scores on both, some countries with similar mean scores on having an intimate friend (Mexico and Turkey) have very different mean scores on social support, and others with similar mean scores on social support (Tanzania and Egypt) have very different mean scores on having an intimate friend.Fig. 2
Mean of social support and proportion of intimate friend

The Gini coefficient measures statistical dispersion or the level of inequality in a country in the level of reported social support, where perfect equality = 0.0 and inequality = 1.0. Figure 3 shows a very strong, inverse relationship (r = −0.88, p < 0.0001) between the mean level of social support in a country and the Gini coefficient for that country (see Table 2). As the Gini coefficient increases, the difference between the highest and lowest responses increases. In countries where people get more help more frequently from relatives and friends, the gap between the highest and lowest levels of support is smaller—most get help more frequently. In countries where people get help less frequently, the gap between highest and lowest is bigger—some have help much more frequently and some much less.Fig. 3
Social support mean and gini coefficient

In summary, similar to previous estimates (Gallup, 2022; WHO Commission of Social Connection, 2024), the majority of people report high levels of CSC. For those who do not, there is high variability across countries in the mean CSC, whether having social support or an intimate friend is stronger, the statistical dispersion of CSC within the country, and the inequality of who lacks CSC.

sec.title: CSC Variability Across Demographic Categories
sec.p: Across the demographic categories, there are global similarities and country differences.

sec.title: Global Similarities
sec.p: Global patterns emerge in the variability across demographic categories (see Tables 3 and 4). The meta-analysis of mean social support shows a U-shaped pattern with age, with the lowest mean being at 40–49 years old. The proportion of people having an intimate friend slightly decreases with older participants, from 84% for the 18–24 years old to 81% for the 70–79 years old. Women report slightly stronger social support (7.50, 95% CI: 7.14, 7.87) than men (7.29, 95% CI: 6.90, 7.69) (p < 0.001), and a slightly higher portion of women (0.85, 95% CI: 0.83, 0.87) are more likely to have an intimate friend than men (0.82, 95% CI: 0.80, 0.85) (p < 0.001), both with overlapping CIs suggesting caution in over-interpreting the differences.

Globally, the married (7.55, 95% CI: 7.15, 7.95) and widowed (7.54, 95% CI: 7.09, 7.99) both report stronger social support than the separated (7.14, 95% CI: 6.80, 7.48) and divorced (7.04, 95% CI: 6.60, 7.48) (p < 0.001). The proportion of the married (0.87, 95% CI: 0.84, 0.89) and domestic partners (0.89, 95% CI: 0.81, 0.94) with an intimate friend is greater than the separated (0.79, 95% CI: 0.75, 0.83), divorced (0.79, 95% CI: 0.75, 0.82), widowed (0.79, 95% CI: 0.76, 0.82), and never married [0.79, 95% CI: 0.76, 0.82). Overall, the retired (7.59, 95% CI: 7.16, 8.02) and students (7.61, 95% CI: 7.27, 7.96) have stronger social support than those working for an employer (7.40, 95% CI: 7.01, 7.79) or who are self-employed (7.42, 95% CI: 7.03, 7.80), which are stronger than the homemakers (7.34, 95% CI: 6.98, 7.71) and the unemployed (6.82, 95% CI: 6.37,7.26). The proportion of the unemployed with an intimate friend (0.78, 95% CI: 0.74, 0.81) is significantly lower than the rest (p < 0.001).

With increasing years of education, participants globally reported having stronger social support and a higher proportion had an intimate friend. Participants who had more frequent religious service attendance also reported higher levels of social support, and a higher proportion had an intimate friend. Those born in the country where they reside reported slightly stronger social support and a higher proportion reported having an intimate friend as compared to those born in another country.

In this section, initial data shows global similarities in higher CSC with increasing years of education, frequency of religious service attendance, being born in the country where you live, being a woman, and being married. There were also global similarities in the U shape of CSC with age and lower CSC for the unemployed.

While the global trend, pooled across countries, is U-shaped for social support, as age increases, in Argentina older participants report slightly higher levels of social support. In Brazil, the strength of social support is substantially higher for older participants, while the proportion with an intimate friend increases in a U-shape, with the lowest for participants 50–59 years old. In Egypt and India, the level of social support generally decreases for older participants, as does the proportion with an intimate friend. In Indonesia, the proportion with an intimate friend tends to decrease for older participants. Israel shows an inverted-U pattern with age for the proportion with an intimate friend, peaking at 30–49 years of age. In the USA, a higher proportion of older participants report having an intimate friend than younger participants, and follows a roughly similar pattern for social support, though this is also slightly higher for those aged 18–24.

While the meta-analysis shows that women report stronger CSC than men, in general, women report lower social support than men in Brazil [women (7.14, 95% CI: 7.04, 7.33), men (7.30, 95% CI (7.20, 7.39) (p = 0.057)]; in Hong Kong [women (6.35, 95% CI: 6.17, 6.53); men (6.45, (6.27, 6.63) (p = 0.022)]; and in Kenya [women (6.88, 95% CI: 6.74, 7.02); men (7.05, 95% CI: 6.91, 7.18) (p = 0.023)].

In Poland, the married have much stronger social support and a much higher proportion with an intimate friend. In Egypt, the divorced have much lower social support (though with a high standard error), and for those who never married social support is much higher. In Australia and Sweden, the widowed have much stronger social support. In Hong Kong, the separated have much higher social support (7.86, 95% CI: 7.24, 8.48), while the widowed have much lower social support (4.67, 95% CI: 3.14, 6.21) (p < 0.001). In Brazil, India, and the USA, the separated have much lower social support. In Israel, the widowed have a much lower proportion with an intimate friend. In Germany and Indonesia, those with a domestic partner reported having much higher social support and higher proportion with an intimate friend. In Tanzania, the never married have much higher social support.

In Australia, students have much lower social support and a lower proportion with an intimate friend. In Egypt, the employed and retired report lower levels of social support than homemakers, students, and the unemployed. In India, the employed for an employer have lower social support. In Indonesia, the retired have a lower level of social support and proportion with an intimate friend. In Israel, the retired have a much lower proportion with an intimate friend.

Globally social support increases with religious service attendance. Argentina shows a more complex pattern, with higher social support at more than 1/week and only a few times a year, and lower social support at 1–4/month, though the proportion with an intimate friend increases with the frequency of religious service attendance. In Egypt, those who never attend report higher levels of social support than those who attend 1–4/month.

In Hong Kong, social support decreases with years of education, with means for those with up to 8 years (6.82, 95% CI: 6.39, 7.25), those with 9–15 years (6.43, 95% CI: 6.27, 6.58), and those with more than 16 years (5.94, 95% CI: 5.70, 6.19) (p < 0.001), as does the proportion with an intimate friend. In South Africa and Tanzania, social support also decreased with years of education, but the proportion with an intimate friend increased with years of education.
In India, those born in another country report lower levels of social support and a higher proportion with an intimate friend than those born in the country.

In summary, while the meta-analysis shows that, overall, there are global similarities in how CSC vary across demographic groups, in some countries the specific distribution of CSC across demographic groups varies greatly in magnitude or direction.

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Close social connections (CSC) are a key determinant of well-being (Helliwell et al., 2017; Holt-Lunstad, 2022; Waldinger & Schulz, 2023). The pre-registered research questions (Ritchie-Dunham et al., 2023b) in this paper sought to describe the demographic distribution of CSC levels across 2 countries. This study underscores the universality of CSC, supporting previous research described in the Introduction, while also highlighting significant differences in mean levels and demographic distributions of CSC across a diverse set of countries, suggesting nuances to the existing literature.

sec.title: Universality of CSC and Significant Differences
sec.p: Confirming previous research (Gallup & Meta, 2023), universally most people report having CSC (Table 2), while a substantial portion of people do not. What differentiates who does and does not have CSC varies significantly across countries and demographic groups. This variability is crucial for understanding the broader social dynamics and the factors that influence social cohesion. These differences are in the mean level of CSC, the relative strength of social support and having an intimate friend, the balance of who has and who lacks CSC, and the demographic categories that have higher or lower CSC.

There are substantial differences in the mean level of CSC in each country (Table 2), with a wide range of social support from relatives and friends when in trouble and in the portion of the population having an intimate friend. The complement to these numbers highlights how large the population is globally with low CSC.

Countries differ in whether they are more supportive of social support or having an intimate friend (Fig. 2), as expressed in the mean level for each in that country and in how these are distributed among their demographic groups. This suggests that the social structures supporting CSC across demographic groups vary greatly across countries.

Countries differ in the equitable balance of who has and who lacks CSC, as expressed in the Gini coefficient for social support (Table 2). While standard deviations around the mean for social support were similar for the surveyed countries, the Gini coefficients (Fig. 3) showed that the imbalance of social support increases as the national mean level of social support decreases. This suggests that as the national mean level of social support decreases, the social conditions that support higher CSC are working for a few and are not working for more.

This study finds differences across countries in the demographic groups that experience higher or lower CSC. First, the portions of a population in each demographic group varied greatly. While age and gender distributions were relatively consistent across countries, by design of the study, other demographic categories such as marital status, employment status, and religious service attendance showed significant variability. This alters the underlying social conditions for CSC in each country. The demographic analysis showed that, overall, CSC varied significantly and in similar ways across age groups, genders, marital statuses, employment statuses, educational levels, and religious service attendance. And for each similarity, there were national examples that countered those patterns. This suggests that while the need for CSC is universal, the ways in which these CSC are formed and maintained can differ greatly depending on cultural and demographic contexts.

sec.title: Alignment with and Deviations from Previous Theory
sec.p: The findings from this first wave of GFS data both align with and highlight deviations from previous theory for each of the demographic categories.

While earlier research suggested a continuous increase of CSC with age, wave 1 of the Global Flourishing Study finds that, in general, the level of CSC starts higher in early adulthood, drops during mid-life in one’s 40 s and 50 s, and increases again later in life, a U shape also found by Blanchflower (2021). There are also countries where CSC increase linearly with age, decrease linearly with age, or peak in the middle range. This might reflect differences in social conditions for working years when adults might have stronger responsibilities for children and parents. Another factor to explore could be large national differences in the social, political, and economic contexts within which these different age cohorts came into adulthood, such as years of war, shifts in gender right, and the predominance of family and religious institutions (Waite et al., 2021). There could also be differences in whether being homebound as an elder increases or decreases social support.

This study suggests nuances needed for future research showing that women have stronger social support than men, with a higher proportion having an intimate friend (Roy et al., 2022), which is reversed in some countries. This might reflect differences in social conditions for the types of work and education available to women, as well as the types of family structures that support stronger or weaker CSC.

This study generally supports research that people build CSC in their place of employment (Dieckhoff & Gash, 2015). Those who are students, employed, and retired had stronger social support and a higher proportion had an intimate friend than those who are homemakers or unemployed. Possible nuances to add to this research come in the variability in which forms of employment had much stronger or weaker CSC in each country.

title.bold: Social (Marital Status, Religious Service Attendance, Education, Immigration)

sec.p: While this study aligns with research suggesting that CSC increased with having a reliable partner in life (Marks & Lambert, 1998), whether married, widowed, or have a domestic partner, countries varied greatly in marital institutions they support. The data also supports the general finding that CSC increases with religious service attendance (Nooney & Woodrum, 2002), as it increases the sense of belonging to a community and aiding others. This theory could expand to explain countries where increased attendance led to lower CSC. While this study confirms earlier research that CSCs, in general, increase with the level of education (Hout, 2012), in some countries it does not, suggesting that the increased access to CSC and development of prosocial behaviors that come with increasing formal education might vary across countries. Counter to the theory that immigrant communities would provide strong CSC, this study showed lower CSC for immigrants.

sec.title: Limitations
sec.p: The limitations of this study include selection and interpretation of the demographic variables used. The demographic variables studied are limited to the demographic variables and countries included in the GFS dataset, which could be expanded in future research to include employment category, socioeconomic level, and family size. Some caution is needed in interpreting cross-national differences, as these may be influenced by matters of translation, different modes of assessment, differing interpretation of items and also of response scales, and seasonal effects arising from data being collected in different countries at different times of the year. Cross-national results should be interpreted with the country-specific cultural norms and contexts in mind. Two different questions were used in this study which can highlight both similar and distinct patterns. To address the issue that the included measures could be interpreted differently within each linguistic group, each question was cognitively tested in each country. This cognitive testing led to the study protocol of treating each measure as non-identical, closely related assessments of each construct, which were analyzed separately for each country (included in the online supplement) and then meta-analyzed, as presented in the main paper (Cowden et al., 2024; Padgett et al., 2025a). This paper describes broad results from the meta-analysis of 22 countries, providing the analysis for each country in the online supplement. The heterogeneity of cultures that happen to be encased or not within similar national borders requires careful attendance to these substantive nuances (Case et al., 2023). Nonetheless, this first wave of the GFS was able to highlight similar patterns and significant differences in how these patterns express levels of CSC across various demographic categories for 22 countries. The associations in this paper are purely descriptive and should not be interpreted causally. Longitudinal analyses will be possible with subsequent waves of the GFS data that might provide further insight into causality.

sec.title: Implications
sec.p: Previous research suggests that incremental changes in the level of social support can have the similar relations to overall life satisfaction as the doubling of GDP per capita (Helliwell et al., 2017). This research strengthens the implications of this finding, showing that the level of CSC can vary by one’s life circumstances. The biggest differences between demographic categories were for employment status, educational levels, and religious service attendance. This suggests that groups who are unemployed, or have low education, or are not involved in a religious community might be specific areas of focus for possible social-support intervention and policies. Policy interventions focused on access to employment and opportunities where workers thrive from work (Clifton & Harter, 2021; Peters et al., 2023, 2024), and on community structures that support regular gatherings (Ide et al., 2023) may be promising. Low levels of CSC for parts of a population also suggest policy interventions to provide supportive environments for those who experience lower levels of CSCs because of their marital status, age, educational level, or having been born out of the country, as well as investment in housing structures and social infrastructure such as adult-education classes or clubs, all which facilitate interpersonal connections.

sec.title: Conclusions
sec.p: This work contributes to research on close social connections by offering insight into how countries differ with regard to the prevalence of social support and having an intimate friend. This study provides initial insights about groups that may need additional assistance in fostering CSC and makes clear that patterns of cultural- and demographic-specific variations exist. In doing so, this work provides a foundational step for future investigation into the social-context factors that systematically support the formation of close social connections. Beyond filling gaps in the empirical literature, this study has important implications due to the significance of social connections for well-being and human flourishing. We hope this study leads to more attention being paid to the issue of CSC across and beyond the academic world, and that the results may provide crucial information for future interventions.
